nus-dbs-rong-li.title: Distinguished Professor, Department of Biological Sciences; Director, Mechanobiology Institute
nus-dbs-rong-li.summary: Professor Rong Li's lab pursues two main research thrusts: cell and tissue aging; and cellular and organismal adaptation. The aging arm studies dynamic changes of cellular components during aging and how they alter the mechanical functions of cells and tissues, aiming to prolong healthy aging and support the repair and regeneration of deteriorated functions. The adaptation arm studies the dynamics of genetic and epigenetic determinants under acute or chronic stress, and the adaptive behaviours that follow — with a long-term view toward preventing cancers associated with chronic inflammatory disease. Prof Li joined NUS in 2019 as the second Director of the Mechanobiology Institute, succeeding Prof Michael Sheetz, and brings more than 25 years of interdisciplinary research on cellular dynamics and mechanics from Johns Hopkins University, where she directed the Center for Cell Dynamics.
roles: Distinguished Professor, Department of Biological Sciences, NUS

ntu-sbs-guillaume-thibault.summary: The Thibault Lab studies how cells sense and respond to stress, with a focus on the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), the organelle responsible for protein folding and lipid regulation. Dysregulation of ER proteostasis is a common feature of metabolic disease, neurodegeneration, chronic inflammation, and aging. We dissect how the unfolded protein response (UPR) decides between cellular adaptation and programmed death, and how distinct stressors — lipid imbalance, oxidative injury, cold, and infection — engage different UPR branches to reshape gene expression, organelle function, and tissue physiology. Using a cross-species strategy spanning yeast, C. elegans, mammalian cells, and mouse models, we investigate how ER stress responses are rewired during cellular senescence, in impaired wound healing and polymicrobial skin infection (including host–Enterococcus faecalis interactions), and in retinal degeneration. We complement wet-lab experiments with single-cell RNA-seq of injured tissues and with bioinformatic pipelines that mine transcriptional signatures across organisms. The group also develops large-scale knowledge graphs (PlantConnectome, Yeast Knowledge Graph) that aggregate the literature into queryable networks, and contributes to synthetic-biology efforts such as SCRaMbLE-based combinatorial pathway screening. Longer term, we aim to identify interventions that selectively restore protective stress responses in disease and aging without collateral damage, and to champion open, reproducible research practices.
roles: Associate Dean (Graduate Studies), College of Science
